source_metadata.title_raw: Gary S. Winuk - A-18-099 - June 29, 2018 - Sacramento

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 29, 2018

Gary S. Winuk
Kaufman Legal Group
A Professional Corporation
621 Capitol Mall, Suite 1900
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-18-099

Dear Mr. Winuk:

     This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Michael Lubic regarding the
conflict of interest code provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are
not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice
we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts
underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

     Regulation 18329.5(a)(2) authorizes the Fair Political Practices Commission (the
"Commission") to provide formal written advice regarding the application of an agency's conflict
of interest code to an individual, if the Commission is the agency's code reviewing body, and if the
individual or his or her representative has first requested a determination from the agency. The
Commission is the code reviewing body for the California Public Employees Retirement System
("CalPERS"), and you have requested a determination from CalPERS on behalf of Mr. Lubic
regarding the question presented. Therefore, we are authorized to provide formal written advice in
response to your request.

QUESTION

     Is Mr. Lubic required to submit Statements of Economic Interests as a consultant subject to
full disclosure as directed by CalPERS considering the scope of the contract work he has already
performed for CalPERS?

CONCLUSION

     Yes. Based on the facts presented, Mr. Lubic must file Statements of Economic Interest as a
consultant subject to full disclosure as directed by CalPERS.

___________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-099
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR

You are the authorized representative of Michael Lubic, an attorney with the K&L Gates
law firm in Los Angeles. On March 21, 2011, CalPERS entered into a contract with K&L Gates for
the provision of legal services, primarily related to bankruptcy issues, covering from that date
through December 31, 2014 (the initial contract). Upon the expiration of the initial contract,
CalPERS entered into a subsequent contract with K&L Gates for substantially similar services from
January 1, 2015 through December 31, 2019 (the second contract). Mr. Lubic was a contract
attorney for CalPERS from 2012 through 2017 pursuant to these contracts. While working for
CalPERS during this time, he was the co-leader of a team that included dozens of lawyers. Mr.
Lubic was the only team member who CalPERS required to file Statements of Economic Interests.

Mr. Lubic's legal specialty lies in bankruptcy and insolvency cases. In 2012, K&L Gates
assigned Mr. Lubic to assist CalPERS with its interests in the City of Stockton bankruptcy and,
shortly thereafter, the City of San Bernardino bankruptcy. From 2012 to 2016, Mr. Lubic's work for
CalPERS was primarily on these two matters. However, Mr. Lubic also worked on two smaller
bankruptcy cases involving CalPERS, provided general advice regarding issues related to the
treatment of CalPERS in municipal bankruptcy cases under state and federal law, and suggested
legislative changes to improve CalPERS' position in future bankruptcies.

You state that CalPERS first advised Mr. Lubic that he was required to file a Statement of
Economic Interests in 2016, over four years after he began working for the agency. At that time, the
City of Stockton case had been resolved, and CalPERS and the City of San Bernardino had entered
into a settlement agreement. You state that all material legal work was completed before Mr. Lubic
received the first request for information from CalPERS. The work performed after receiving the
request for information was limited to monitoring the plan of adjustment filed by the City of San
Bernardino to confirm its compliance with the settlement agreement between the City and
CalPERS.

Mr. Lubic immediately sought out your firm's representation upon receiving CalPERS'
notice that he was required to file Statements of Economic Interests and subject to full disclosure.
You state that Mr. Lubic would not have accepted the contract work from CalPERS had he known
that he would have to file Statements of Economic Interests. Mr. Lubic has no current contract work
with, and does not contemplate doing any future work for, a state or local government. Near the end
of 2016, you requested that CalPERS limit Mr. Lubic's required disclosure under its Conflict of
Interest Code. CalPERS declined the request.

You state that Mr. Lubic understands and appreciates the importance of public disclosure
and avoiding conflicts of interest. However, for family reasons unrelated to his work performed on
behalf of CalPERS, he does not wish to provide full disclosure of his financial interests which are
not related to that work. Accordingly, you have requested that the Commission, CalPERS' code
reviewing body, limit Mr. Lubic's required disclosure under CalPERS' Conflict of Interest Code.

File No. A-18-099
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Toward that end, every agency
must adopt and promulgate a conflict of interest code. (Section 87300.)

A conflict of interest code must be formulated at the most decentralized level possible
(Section 87301); enumerate the positions within the agency, including consultant positions, that
involve making or participation in the making of decisions which may have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on any financial interest (Section 87302(a)); and set forth the
specific types of investments, business positions, real property interests, or sources of income which
are reportable for each enumerated position (ibid).

The Act defines:"public official" to include "every member, officer, employee or consultant
of a state or local government agency" (Section 82048), and requires a public official to file
Statements of Economic Interests, annually and upon assuming or leaving office, disclosing his or
her personal assets and interests that may be affected during the performance of the official's duties
(Sections 87200 to 87350). At issue is whether Mr. Lubic, as a consultant for CalPERS, is subject to
full disclosure under CalPERS' Conflict of Interest Code.

CalPERS' Conflict of Interest Code

With respect to its inclusion of "consultants," CalPERS' Conflict of Interest Code provides
in pertinent part as follows:

Consultants/New positions shall be included in the list of designated positions
and shall disclose pursuant to the broadest disclosure category in the code,
subject to the following limitation: The General Counsel may determine in
writing that a particular consultant or new position, although a 'designated
position,' is hired to perform a range of duties that is limited in scope, and
thus, is not required to fully comply with the disclosure requirements in this
section. Such written determination shall include a description of the
consultant's or new position's duties and, based upon that description, a
statement of the extent of disclosure requirements.

Thus, a consultant for CalPERS is subject to the "broadest disclosure category in the code" unless
the agency's General Counsel determines in writing that the consultant "is hired to perform a range
of duties that is limited in scope."

The Initial Contract

K&L Gates' initial contract to provide legal services to CalPERS from March 21, 2011
through December 31, 2014, provides as follows with respect to "Services to Be Performed":

File No. A-18-099
Page No. 4

Contractor agrees to provide legal services to the Board, as requested by the
Board's General Counsel or his designee, in connection with issues pertaining
to bankruptcy. Such services may include, but are not limited to, the following:

i) Respond to the opinion requests from CalPERS Board and staff.

ii) Represent the Board in state and federal judicial proceedings, including
bankruptcy proceedings.

iii) Monitor, advice and report on state and federal judicial proceedings,
including bankruptcy proceedings.

iv) Prepare and submit periodic reports describing the status of all matters
assigned hereunder before the Contractor.

v) Other legal services as requested by the CalPERS General Counsel or his
designee.

Contractor acknowledges that the Board may retain other attorneys and/or law
firms, including the California Attorney General's Office, to provide legal
services. The Board's General Counsel shall retain sole discretion over the
selection and volume of matters referred to the Contractor.

Thus, the initial contract required K&L Gates to perform specified work pertaining to bankruptcy
issues, respond to CalPERS' opinion requests, and provide CalPERS with other legal services.

The Second Contract

Upon the expiration of the initial contract, K&L Gates entered into the second contract. The
second contract's "Services to Be Performed" section is substantially similar to the initial contract.
The first five duties enumerated therein are nearly identical to those set forth in the initial contract,
and the last paragraph of this section in the second contract, relating to other attorneys or law firms
and discretion over matters assigned, is reproduced verbatim from the initial contract. The
following two additional duties are included in the second contract's "Services to Be Performed":

vi. Prepare and provide the following training to CalPERS legal staff at no
cost: (i) include CalPERS legal staff on the mailing list for client publications
prepared by Contractor; (ii) regularly provide training/CLE opportunities for
CalPERS legal staff in several formats including in person, video-conference
and webinars; and (iii) present a full day, multidisciplinary CLE on topics of
interest to CalPERS, which will be conducted by up to three of Contractor's
lawyers (estimated total time commitment for such a presentation is
approximately 24 hours).

vii. Ensure transfer of information between Key Persons to avoid duplication
of services or loss of efficiency.

File No. A-18-099
Page No. 5

Thus, the second contract required K&L Gates to perform all the duties enumerated in the initial contract and the additional duties of providing specified legal training for CalPERS legal staff and ensuring the transfer of information between key persons.

Interpretation of CalPERS' Conflict of Interest Code

As noted above, under CalPERS' Conflict of Interest Code, a consultant is subject to the "broadest disclosure category in the code" unless CalPERS' General Counsel determines in writing that the consultant is "hired to perform a range of duties that is limited in scope." You requested that CalPERS limit Mr. Lubic's required disclosure to his financial interests related to his work on behalf of CalPERS, and CalPERS declined the request.

Although the CalPERS' contracts with K&L Gates charged the law firm with duties relating to bankruptcy issues, they also assigned it other duties not associated with those issues. For instance, under both contracts, K&L Gates is required to respond to opinion requests from CalPERS and to provide other legal services requested by the CalPERS' General Counsel. The second contract additionally requires K&L Gates to provide the CalPERS legal staff with wide-ranging legal training not limited to bankruptcy issues. Therefore, the express duties assigned to K&L Gates in the initial and second contracts are not limited in scope.

Mr. Lubic worked as a contract attorney for CalPERS from 2012 through 2017. During this time, he was a co-leader of a team of dozens of lawyers, and he was the only member of his team required to file Statements of Economic Interests. Mr. Lubic's work for CalPERS from 2012 to 2016 was primarily focused on assisting CalPERS with its interests in the City of Stockton and City of San Bernardino bankruptcies. However, he also worked on smaller bankruptcy cases, provided general advice regarding issues related to the treatment of CalPERS in municipal bankruptcy cases, suggested legislative changes to improve CalPERS' position in future bankruptcies, and monitored the City of San Bernardino's plan of adjustment to confirm its compliance with the settlement agreement between the City and CalPERS.

As noted above, the Act requires an agency's Conflict of Interest Code to enumerate its positions, including consultant positions, that involve taking part in certain governmental decisions, and to specify the types of investments, business positions, real property interests, or sources of income which are reportable for each enumerated position. (Section 87302(a).) Also noted above, CalPERS' Conflict of Interest Code enumerates consultants and provides that a consultant is subject to full disclosure unless CalPERS' General Counsel determines that the consultant was "hired to perform a range of duties that is limited in scope." As a party to its contracts with K&L Gates, CalPERS is uniquely situated to determine the scope of the duties Mr. Lubic was hired to perform, and CalPERS's General Counsel has declined to determine those duties limited in scope.

Therefore, based on the facts presented, the duties that CalPERS hired Mr. Lubic to perform were not limited in scope. Accordingly, we conclude that Mr. Lubic was a consultant subject to full disclosure under CalPERS' Conflict of Interest Code with respect to his past contract work already performed on its behalf, and therefore he must file Statements of Economic Interests as directed by CalPERS.

File No. A-18-099
Page No. 6

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Acting General Counsel

[signature]

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl
